coredata.dc:title: Sustainable photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water from biomass carbon dots decorated resorcinol–formaldehyde resin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Haoyuan
Li, Linhui
Tang, Yuanhao
Cao, Chen
Zhang, Yatao
Guo, Feng
Shi, Weilong
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a promising green strategy to replace conventional industrial production methods. Herein, ginkgo biloba-derived biomass carbon dots (BCDs) were successfully loaded on the surface of resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin by a simple hydrothermal method to form a BCDs/RF composite photocatalyst, which enabled efficient and sustainable photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water without the sacrificial agents and bubbling O2. The optimal H2O2 yield of the BCDs/RF-5 composite catalyst was 1430.31 μmol h−1 g−1 with the solar energy chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency was up to 1.1 %, which was 1.16 times higher than that of pure RF (1222.59 μmol h−1 g−1). A series of testing results illustrate that the BCDs in the BCDs/RF composite not only acted as electron acceptors to enhance the photo-generated charge separation efficiency of RF resin, but also effectively inhibited the decomposition of H2O2, and on the other hand improved the yield of photocatalytic H2O2 production. This study provides a new strategy for the design and synthesis of RF-based photocatalysts and expands the application of biomass materials in photocatalytic generation of H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic
H2O2 production
Biomass
Carbon dots
Resorcinol-formaldehyde resin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006057 and 21906072 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023\u202F M743178 ), Jiangsu Province Industry-University-Research Cooperation Project ( BY20231482 ) and the Open Fund of the Key Laboratory of Solar Cell electrode Materials in China Petroleum and Chemical Industry ( 2024A093 ).

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006057 and 21906072), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023 M743178), Jiangsu Province Industry-University-Research Cooperation Project (BY20231482) and the Open Fund of the Key Laboratory of Solar Cell electrode Materials in China Petroleum and Chemical Industry (2024A093).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images (a) RF and (b) BCDs/RF. (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of BCDs/RF-5. EDS spectra of (e) RF and (f) BCDs/RF-5.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of RF and BCDs/RF composites. XPS spectra of the RF and BCDs/RF-5: (c) survey spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s and (f) O 1 s.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of all samples. (b) Band gap curves of RF and BCDs/RF-5. (c) Mott-Schottky plots and (d) energy band structure diagrams of RF and BCDs/RF-5.

(a) Concentration of H2O2 produced by RF systems with different amounts of BCDs under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). (b) Photocatalytic H2O2 production over RF and BCDs/RF-5 under different scavengers. Photocatalytic H2O2 production under different (c) atmospheres. (d) pH values and (e) anion/cation ions. (f) H2O2 decomposition tests of BCDs/RF-5 catalyst under visible light/in dark. (g) H2O2 formation (K f) and decomposition (K d) rate constant of without/with adding BCDs/RF-5. (h) Solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency of BCDs/RF-5 photocatalyst. (i) Cycles of photocatalytic H2O2 generation performance by BCDs/RF-5.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses, (b) EIS plots, (c) PL spectra and (d) TRPL decay curves of RF and BCDs/RF-5.

(a) H2O2 selectivity curves of RF and BCDs/RF-5 (inset is the corresponding number (n) of electro transfer as a function of the applied potential). (b) ESR spectra of DMPO‐•O2 − over RF and BCDs/RF-5 samples. (c) Temperature programmed O2 desorption (TPD-O2) profiles of RF and BCDs/RF-5. (d) Changes in the corresponding absorbance of active species in-situ FT-IR spectra with a periodic light irradiation for 14 min.
Photocatalytic mechanism of H2O2 production based on BCDs/RF composite.
Schematic diagram of preparation process over BCDs/RF composite photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Sustainable photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water from biomass carbon dots decorated resorcinol–formaldehyde resin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a promising green strategy to replace conventional industrial production methods. Herein, ginkgo biloba-derived biomass carbon dots (BCDs) were successfully loaded on the surface of resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin by a simple hydrothermal method to form a BCDs/RF composite photocatalyst, which enabled efficient and sustainable photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water without the sacrificial agents and bubbling O2. The optimal H2O2 yield of the BCDs/RF-5 composite catalyst was 1430.31 μmol h−1 g−1 with the solar energy chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency was up to 1.1 %, which was 1.16 times higher than that of pure RF (1222.59 μmol h−1 g−1). A series of testing results illustrate that the BCDs in the BCDs/RF composite not only acted as electron acceptors to enhance the photo-generated charge separation efficiency of RF resin, but also effectively inhibited the decomposition of H2O2, and on the other hand improved the yield of photocatalytic H2O2 production. This study provides a new strategy for the design and synthesis of RF-based photocatalysts and expands the application of biomass materials in photocatalytic generation of H2O2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an important chemical with a wide range of applications in the fields of disinfection, bleaching, chemical synthesis, and environmental treatment owing to its powerful oxidative properties, especially in the fields of health care and food safety, where it is regarded as an ideal disinfectant since its non-toxic by-products and highly effective bactericidal effect [1,2]. Nevertheless, the traditional method of H2O2 production mainly relies on the anthraquinone method, which is more efficient but suffers from defects such as high energy consumption, high cost, environmental pollution, and a complex production process with high equipment requirements, and it is an urgent need to develop a more environmentally friendly and efficient alternative method [3,4]. Photocatalytic H2O2 production using artificial photosynthesis is an ideal method to produce high-value chemicals by simply using sunlight, water and oxygen, which is one of the most environmentally friendly alternative technologies for the H2O2 preparation [5–7].

Up to now, most metal-based photocatalysts have been reported to be used for H2O2 production, such as metal oxides (ZnO [8–10], TiO2 [11], WO3 [12–14], etc.), metal sulphides (CdS [15,16], ZnS [17], etc.), and metal–organic frameworks (MOF, UIO-66 [18], MIL-125 [19], etc.). Nevertheless, there are some irreconcilable problems with metal-based materials as follows: (i) H2O2 can be easily decompose on the surface of metal oxides or metal particles even under dark conditions [20]; (ii) the synthetic methods of metal-based materials were carried out with complex synthesis, which increase loss of resources and prime cost in the production process.[21]; (iii) metal-based materials, especially metal sulfides, tend to overflow with metal ions, which can cause secondary pollution of the water environment [22]. Consequently, it is still a significant challenge to search for non-metallic photocatalytic materials with green synthesis process for photocatalytic H2O2 production.

In recent years, resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin, as a novel non-metallic photocatalytic material, has attracted the attention of researchers with unique benzene-quinone π-coupling donor–acceptor (D-A) unit structure, which could drive the photocatalytic H2O2 production from water [23]. In addition, RF resin could share a strong visible-light absorption in the range of > 600 nm due to the narrow band gap (∼ 2.0 eV) [24,25]. However, some inherent defects of RF resins hinder further enhancement of their photocatalytic activity, where the specific surface area of RF resins makes them unsuitable for mass transfer activity catalytic sites [26,27], and the low photo-induced charges’ mobility rate [28,29]. There is thereby an urgent need but it is still a significant challenge to rationally design and delicately regulation of RF resin for further enhancing its photocatalytic H2O2 production performance.

Carbon dots (CDs), especially biomass CDs (BCDs), are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with low-cost, easy synthesize, high biocompatibility, and usable waste and renewable materials are in line with the concept of sustainable development [30–35]. Due to their water solubility, abundant functional groups, excellent photo-absorption and carriers’ generation, and interesting physical, optical and chemical properties, CDs have been demonstrated to be well suited for combining with other photocatalysts to enhance their photocatalytic activity [36–41]. For instance, Yu et al. modified sulfur-defective ZnIn2S4 by loaded CDs, which can act as a stimulant so that the direction of charge redistribution effectively affects the number of active sites in the composite system [42]. In addition, Chang et al. prepared a novel hydroxyapatite/n-doped carbon dots/Ag3PO4 (HA/N-CDs/Ag3PO4) composite via using a simple hydrothermal and in situ ion-exchange reaction, in which CDs and Ag co-catalysts act together to inhibit electron-hole pairs’ recombination and improvement of the stability of the composite photocatalyst [43]. These features suggest that the CDs or BCDs can be considered as ideal active site as electron acceptors to combine RF resin semiconductor for the construction of composite system with applicability in high-efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production.

In this work, BCDs extracted from Ginkgo biloba leaves were successfully coated on the surface of RF resin by a simple hydrothermal method to form a BCDs/RF composite photocatalyst, which achieved efficient and sustainable photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water without the need of sacrificial agent and bubbling O2. The optimal H2O2 yield of the BCDs/RF-5 composite catalyst was1430.31 μmol h−1 g−1 with the solar energy chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency was up to 1.1 %, which was (1.16 times higher than that of pure RF (1222.59 μmol h−1 g−1). A series of characterization results revealed that the BCDs in the BCDs/RF composite not only acted as an electron acceptor to transfer electrons from the RF for improving the charge separation, but also effectively inhibited the decomposition of H2O2, further enhancing the yield of photocatalytic H2O2 production. This study provides a new strategy for the design and synthesis of RF-based photocatalysts and expands the application of biomass materials in the photocatalytic generation of H2O2.
Resorcinol (C6H6O2, AR), formaldehyde solution (CH2O, 37 wt%) and ammonium hydroxide solution (NH3·H2O, 28 wt% ∼ 30 wt%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass carbon dots (BCDs) solution
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous work, biomass carbon dots (labelled as BCDs) were synthesized from ginkgo biloba leaves via a representative hydrothermal method. Briefly speaking, 5 g of fresh ginkgo biloba and 100 mL of deionized water were added to a 150 mL reactor and heated at 200 ℃ for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid product obtained from the reaction was centrifuged to form the brown-yellow CDs solution with the concentration of 22 mg/mL.

The resorcinol–formaldehyde (denoted as RF) resin was prepared according to a previously reported method. Specifically, a white colloidal suspension was obtained by adding 792 mg of C6H6O2, 1080 μL CH2O solution and 460 μL NH3·H2O to 60 mL of deionized water and stirring at room temperature. Whereafter, the as-prepared suspension above was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 250 ℃ for 24 h. After natural cooling, the red target solids were thoroughly washed with water and ethanol and further dried under vacuum at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

The preparation process of BCDs/RF composite photocatalysts was the same as that of the RF, and the synthetic procedures are described in Scheme 1 . The difference is that when C6H6O2, CH2O solution and NH3·H2O are dissolved in deionized water, a certain volume (3, 5 and 7 mL) of the BCDs solution is introduced and completely mixed. Finally, BCDs/RF composites with different BCDs loading were obtained by subsequent hydrothermal treatment, and samples were named as BCDs/RF-3, BCDs/RF-5 and BCDs/RF-7, respectively.
In addition, corresponding characterizations, detailed experimental procedures for photo-electrochemical tests, photocatalytic H2O2 production experiments are provided in the Supporting Information.

The morphology and elemental composition of the resulting RF and BCDs/RF photocatalysts were meticulously analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the RF resin exhibits a spherical morphology with an approximate diameter of 300 nm, while after coating on the BCDs on the surface of RF resin, the spherical morphology almost unchanged. However, the presence of BCDs was not observed by SEM technique, which is mainly due to its smaller size. In addition, the TEM image of as-prepared BCDs was provided in Fig. S1a, which indicates that BCDs possesses the circular point morphology and uniform centralized size distribution (Fig. S1b). To further observe the presence of BCDs in the composite material, TEM and HRTEM images of BCDs/RF were carried out, and it can be seen that multiple black particles can be discerned, scattered on the surface of the RF micrometer sphere (Fig. 1c and d). Additionally, energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis also sideways reflects that the composite contains a significantly higher carbon content compared to pure RF (Fig. 1e and f), further corroborating the successful incorporation of BCDs into the RF resin.

In the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peak of RF appears at 2θ = 20°, belonging to the graphite carbon (002) crystal plane, indicating the presence of π-accumulation aromatic hydrocarbons [44]. Not only that, the XRD pattern of BCDs displaced in Fig. S2 exhibits a broad peak at 20°-30°, indicating that the carbon core of BCDs has the graphite structure, and this similar structure can be observed from BCDs/RF samples. In addition, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. 2b confirms that the as-obtained samples are the typical phenolic resin structure. A broad peak in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 can be found in the BCDs/RF samples, representing –OH vibrations, which originate from hydroxyl groups on the surface of the BCDs. To further determine the existence of BCDs successfully loaded on RF, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on the composite. The XPS survey spectra of the BCDs/RF showed mainly C, O and N elements, with a significant increase in N content after the introduction of the BCDs (the N element in the pristine RF was derived from ammonia in the synthesis step). (Fig. 2c). As presented in Fig. 2d, the C 1 s XPS spectra of the two samples were deconvolved into three peaks at 284.6, 286.2 and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C = C, C-O and C = O, respectively [45]. Fig. 1e exhibits the O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of RF and BCDs/RF-5, and all samples in the range of 536–528 eV, deconvolved into two peaks at 533.1 and 531.2 eV, representing C-O and C = O respectively. Finally, the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f) confirmed C = N-C (399.0 eV), N-H (400.2 eV eV), and C-N-C (401.6 eV) [46].

The influence of BCDs on RF absorbance was studied by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy, revealing that the absorption edge of RF is about 750 nm, while the absorption edge was significantly redshifted after introducing BCDs, which increased from 750 nm to 787 nm (Fig. 3 a). These results indicated that the load of BCDs effectively improved the light absorption capacity of RF resin. Fig. 3b provides the band gap values of as-prepared samples determined according to the UV–vis diffusion spectral conversion of the Kubelka-Munk function. The calculated band gaps for RF and BCDs/RF are 1.96 and 2.05 eV, respectively. To further investigate the band structures of RF and BCDs/RF-5, Mott-Schottky (M−S) curves with three different frequencies of 800, 1000 and 1200 Hz were measured and displayed in Fig. 3c, resulting in −0.57 and −0.58 eV for RF and BCDs/RF-5, respectively, and the slopes of the M−S plots for all samples are positive, which is characteristic of the n-type semiconductor [47]. According to the obtained conduction band (CB) position and band gap values, the band structure diagrams of RF and BCDs/RF-5 are presented in Fig. 3d. Obviously, the positions of the CB and valence band (VB) for the pure RF and BCDs/RF composite meet the thermodynamic conditions for photocatalytic H2O2 production. From the energy band structure, it can be seen that the loading of BCDs enhances the reduction ability of the composite catalysts, as well as strengthens the light-absorbing ability, broadens the light-absorbing range, thus effectively enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the RF resin.

The photocatalytic activity of the catalysts for H2O2 production was evaluated by dispersing the catalysts in pure water and irradiating the catalysts (25 mg) under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). Fig. 4 a demonstrates the photocatalytic H2O2 production activity of RF and BCDs/RF composites with different BCDs loading contents. Based on the standard H2O2 concentration curves in Fig. S3, we calculated the yield of photocatalytic H2O2 production in the synthesized samples. Notably, all BCDs/RF composites exhibited higher photocatalytic H2O2 yields compared to pristine RF (383.17), whereas BCDs/RF-5 demonstrated the highest H2O2 production (576.34), which was 1.5 times higher than that of pure RF, which suggests that the introduction of BCDs can effectively improve the photocatalytic H2O2 activity of pristine RF resin, reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs and improving electrons’ utilization. To further demonstrate that the introduction of BCDs reduced the electron-hole pair recombination of the BCDs/RF composite, the H2O2 yield in the presence of different sacrifice agents were tested. With the addition of AgNO3 as an electron trapping agent, no H2O2 was produced in both RF and BCDs/RF samples, which indicates that electrons are the main photo-generated excitons for the H2O2 production, whereas the addition of holes’ sacrificing agent (EtOH) still resulted in the generation of H2O2, but the improvement rate of RF has a higher yield compared to BCDs/RF, revealing that to some extent that the combination of BCDs and RF is effective in reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the composite photocatalytic system. In addition, the production of BCDs/RF photocatalytic H2O2 was also measured under different air conditions, with the highest production rate of H2O2 in O2, and the decrease in air production was almost completely inhibited under N2 conditions (Fig. 4c), indicating that H2O2 was formed through oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) rather than other paths. Since the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 involves the transfer of protons and electrons [48], an acidic medium is usually preferred to facilitate the efficient photocatalytic generation of BCDs/RF-5 in neutral to acidic environments (Fig. 4d). Not only that, in 0.1 M different kinds of ion environment (Fig. 4e), BCDs/RF catalyst can still maintain stable activity, which can effectively indicate the stronger adaptability of the catalyst in complex working environment. The H2O2 yield was tested in competition between the formation and decomposition routes, so the formation rate (K f) and the decomposition rate (K d) were determined, as given in Fig. 4f and g, RF and BCDs/RF-5 decomposed 14.36 and 7.28 μM H2O2 at the same concentration in the dark, thus the Kf obtained by calculating RF and BCDs/RF-5 were 191.58 and 288.17 μM−1, respectively and the corresponding Kd values were measured to be 7.18 and 3.14 μM−1, respectively. The above data can indicate that the amount of H2O2 after loading the BCDs, proving that the introduction of BCDs in composite effectively inhibited the decomposition of H2O2 by the overall catalyst in the system. In addition, the solar-chemical energy conversion (SCC) efficiency of RF and BCDs/RF-5 were 0.79 % and 1.1 %, respectively, which further showed that the load of BCDs effectively improved the solar energy utilization of RF. In addition, the stability is also the important evaluation index of the catalyst (Fig. 4i), and after four cycles of experimental testing, production of H2O2 is almost the same, displaying the the BCDs/RF composite catalyst exhibits the stable photocatalytic activity.

A series of photoelectrochemical characterizations were carried out to investigate the reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 activity of BCDs/RF composite. From the analysis of the charge migration dynamics, the electronic transfer advantages of BCDs/RF composite photocatalysts were also confirmed by transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [49]. The photocurrent intensity of BCDs/RF-5 was significantly higher than that of RF, and its resistance was significantly shorter than that of RF (Fig. 5 a and b), which indicate that the modification of biomass BCDs in BCDs/RF composite not only enhances the reduction potential of RF, but also improves the photocurrent response dramatically, resulting in the higher photo-generated carrier separation and transfer efficiency. In addition, the intensity of the photocurrent decayed significantly without an increase in light time, suggesting that as-prepared BCDs/RF composite also possesses excellent photochemical stability. To furthermore investigate the effect of BCDs loading on the charge separation ability of RF materials, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were tested, and as given in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs/RF appeared to be significantly reduced compared with pristine RF resin, indicating that the introduction of BCDs in composite effectively suppressed the photo-generated carriers’ recombination. Then, the photo-generated carrier kinetics were studied in depth using time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy according to the Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)
y =
y 0
+
A 1
exp
-
t
τ 1
+
A 2
exp
-
t
τ 2
+
A 3
exp
-
t
τ 3
where the average lifetime of excitons in BCDs/RF (2.32 ns) is significantly longer than that in RF (0.69 ns), which also intuitively proves that the addition of BCDs in BCDs/RF composites can greatly inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs under irradiation.

The selectivity of the ORR is crucial for the production of H2O2, thus the effect of BCDs in BCDs/RF on selectivity and transferred electrons of RF was investigated by electrochemical tests on a rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE, Fig. S5). The number of electrons transferred and the H2O2 selectivity of RF and BCDs/RF-5 were obtained by calculations, as can be clearly seen in Fig. 6 a, where the number of electrons transferred on BCDs/RF-5 is nearer to 2 under the same conditions and its H2O2 selectivity for 2e− ORR is higher. In order to determine the active species in the photocatalytic process, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were measured as provided in Fig. 6b and Fig. S6, both samples have a single Lorentzian line, whereas the BCDs/RF possesses the strongest intensity of the •O2 − signals, while the signal of the •OH is the weakest (the •OH is generally originated from the decomposition of H2O2 [50]), which suggests that it produces more •O2 − and less •OH, which proves that the introduction of BCDs effectively enhanced the ORR process and inhibited the decomposition of H2O2. Moreover, temperature-programmed O2 (TPD-O2) desorption revealed that BCDs/RF-5 exhibited a stronger O2 adsorption capacity and stronger O2 surface binding strength than RF, and the improved O2 adsorption characteristics could promote ORR and contribute positively to the increase in H2O2 production activity (Fig. 6c). Finally, the precise mechanism was investigated by in situ Fourier transform infrared (in-situ FTIR), and the spectra of photocatalytic H2O2 produced by the BCDs/RF photocatalytic system under water vapor and continuous O2 flow conditions are given in Fig. 6d. The 1200, 1253, and 1457 cm−1 intensities at long irradiation time intervals are attributed to the * OO, * OOH, and * HOOH vibrations of the surface adsorbed active species, respectively [51], which clearly and intuitively illustrate the pathway of H2O2 formation on the surface of the BCDs/RF: the two-electron ORR process.

Based on the analysis above, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of H2O2 production from BCDs/RF composite was proposed and given in Fig. 7 . Under visible light irradiation, the BCDs/RF composite can be photoexcited to produce electron-hole pairs due to the semiconductor property of RF resin. Afterwards, the photo-generated electrons on the CB of RF resin could transfer to the surface of BCDs, which exhibits outstanding electron reservoir activity, promoting the separation of photo-induced charges. Then photo-electrons in BCDs can reduce O2 to ·O2 − (−0.046 eV for O2/·O2 −) in aqueous solution, further forming H2O2 though the ORR process. Meanwhile, the presence of oxygen-containing groups (such as –COOH and –OH) in BCDs could also inhibit the decomposition of H2O2 (holes oxidation) [52], which enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the composite system from another perspective.

In summary, ginkgo biloba-derived BCDs was successfully coated on the surface of RF resin to form BCDs/RF composite photocatalyst via a simple hydrothermal method for achieving efficient and sustainable photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water without sacrifice agent and bubbling O2. A series of test results exhibit that BCDs in BCDs/RF composite could not only act as an electron acceptor to transfer electrons from RF to the surface of BCDs, which effectively improved the charge separation ability of BCDs/RF composite system, but also effectively inhibited the decomposition of H2O2, which on the other hand improved the yield of photocatalytic generated H2O2. The present study provides a new strategy for the design and synthesis of RF-based photocatalysts and extends the application of biomass materials in photocatalytic H2O2 production.

Haoyuan Qin: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Linhui Li: Data curation. Yuanhao Tang: Data curation. Chen Cao: Data curation. Yatao Zhang: Resources. Feng Guo: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Weilong Shi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006057 and 21906072), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023 M743178), Jiangsu Province Industry-University-Research Cooperation Project (BY20231482) and the Open Fund of the Key Laboratory of Solar Cell electrode Materials in China Petroleum and Chemical Industry (2024A093).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.116058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide evolution: What is the most effective strategy?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Yu, C. Hu, L. Bai, N. Tian, Y. Zhang, H. Huang, Photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide evolution: What is the most effective strategy?, Nano Energy, 104 (2022) 107906-107935.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Abundant Edge Active Sites-Modified High-Crystalline g-C3N5 for Hydrogen Peroxide Production from Pure-Water via a Quasi-Homogeneous Photocatalytic Process
